column1: 2004-08-18窶撒hrough the smoked glass of the front door I saw a man fall. I heard the muffled thumps as he fell to the ground. My old man and I rushed to the back gate and barged it open. When had I put down the gardening tools we had brought with us I went in to see my old man helping ﾃ｢竄ｬﾋ廱ﾃ｢竄ｬ邃｢ up and getting him to a chair.  I first met J about a year ago. He
column2: being the father of my old manﾃ｢竄ｬ邃｢s partner
column3: is the equivalent of a grandfather in my interesting family. Since I first met him I have probably seen him a dozen or so times. Weﾃ｢竄ｬ邃｢ve had family meals together
column4: heﾃ｢竄ｬ邃｢s more than once been round to fix the family car (which he helped buy)
column7: weﾃ｢竄ｬ邃｢ve been round his to lend a hand. I believe he was with the RAF in WWII
column9: until he became a mechanic. Heﾃ｢竄ｬ邃｢s old school and he looks after his family. Based on my limited experiences of him I judge him to be a decent man.  J has cancer. I donﾃ｢竄ｬ邃｢t believe it to be terminal but the medication he is on is strength sapping. It induces drowsiness and a lack of hunger
column10: which makes it difficult for him to move around and consequently his muscles weaken through inactivity.  I never really planned on living beyond my mid-forties. A biological grandfather died of a cardiac episode at that age and I
column12: have had the occasional spate of chest pains. The way I saw it I might not live too much longer then 45 based on my genetic inheritance. Fair enough: weﾃ｢竄ｬ邃｢re all born to die at the end of the day and I figured all I would have to look forward to at that age would be my prime behind me and a pension funded descent in front of me.  I mentioned this in conversation to my old man in the garden the other day. He stopped
column14: and told me that people on their deathbeds would fight to just get outside and look at the sun on such a day. Fight just to enjoy the pleasantly warm air.  Today I saw a man struggle to answer his own door and to get up and down his own stairs. Struggle to do what little he could. I got put of bed after either sleeping or dosing for 11 hours. I have lazed about for pretty much all of the holidays
column15: not spending any effort to do hardly anything. A man struggled to do little more than open his own door.  I reckon thereﾃ｢竄ｬ邃｢s an awful lot of nobility in that manﾃ｢竄ｬ邃｢s struggle
column16: no matter how minor an event it may have been for. It shamefully reminds me I am but a child and that no matter how old or weak someone can become
column17: if they struggle to do whatever they can then they are still a man. I hope to be able to struggle for so little if I ever am in that position as opposed to allowing lascitude to overcome me.  Excuse the gender discrimination.
